request.question: Who was the singer of She's Not Just a Pretty Face and Forever and for Always?

title: Impatient (Anna Abreu song)
Dolly Sinatra
She's Not Just a Pretty Face
Just a Pretty Face?
Forever and for Always
Face Facts

sentences: "Impatient" is a song by Finnish singer Anna Abreu from her third studio album, "Just a Pretty Face?
Abreu co-wrote the song with longtime collaborator Rauli Eskolin (known professionally as Rake), and Patric Sarin.
Eskolin also produced the song, while Sarin provided additional backing vocals.
"Impatient" is a Pop ballad.
The song was released on 9 November 2009 in Finland, as the album's second single.
Dolly Sinatra (born Natalina Maria Vittoria Garaventa; December 26, 1896 – January 6, 1977) was the mother of American singer Frank Sinatra.
She was born in Lumarzo (Province of Genoa), in northern Italy; she immigrated to the United States when she was two months old.
When she was a child, her pretty face earned her the nickname "Dolly".
"She's Not Just a Pretty Face" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain.
It was the fourth country single from her album "Up!
The song was written by Robert John "Mutt" Lange and Twain.
Twain had announced that "Nah!"
would be the next single after "Forever and for Always", but "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was released instead.
It became Twain's 15th top ten single on Billboard Country.
The song was originally to be released to the UK as a pop single on March 24, 2004, but that plan was cancelled.
It was however released to Eastern Europe as the fourth single, where it did reasonably well in some countries.
"She's Not Just a Pretty Face" debuted in North America on October 6, 2003.
Despite reaching the top ten, "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was not included in Twain's 2004 "Greatest Hits" CD.
The song was also nominated for Best Female Country Vocal Performance at the 2005 Grammy Awards.
The song was also performed on some highly rated TV shows, "The Oprah Winfrey Show", the 2003 Country Music Awards, and the 2003 Billboard Music Awards, all helping it land in the top ten.
Just a Pretty Face?
is the third studio album by Finnish singer Anna Abreu, released in Finland by RCA on October 21, 2009.
The album was preceded by the lead single "Music Everywhere" and followed by the promotional singles "Impatient" and "Slam".
"Forever and for Always" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain and it was the third country single from her fourth studio album "Up!
The song was written by Mutt Lange and Twain.
The single was released in the United States radio on April 7, 2003.
The video debuted on Country Music Television on April 26, 2003.
The song was also used for Febreze: Scentstories promotion, in which Twain took part.
In 2006, "Forever and for Always" was certified gold for 500,000 digital downloads in the U.S. by the RIAA.
Face Facts is a 1980 album from glam rock band T. Roth and Another Pretty Face.
Bon Jovi drummer Tico Torres plays on the album.

On to Victory is the ninth studio album recorded by the English rock band Humble Pie and the first with a new lineup including vocalist and guitarist Steve Marriott, Drummer Jerry Shirley, vocalist and guitarist Bobby Tench from The Jeff Beck Group, and American bassist Anthony "Sooty" Jones.
They recorded "Fool for a Pretty Face", which Marriott had written earlier and the song proved good enough for them to secure a recording contract with Atco in 1980.
In UK their material was released by Jet Records, owned by the former Small Faces manager Don Arden.

The album peaked #60 on the "Billboard" 200 album chart "Fool for a Pretty Face" was released as a single and reached #58 on the US singles charts and a promotional tour followed as part of "The Rock'n Roll Marathon", supporting Ted Nugent and Aerosmith
Never Trust a Pretty Face is the third studio album by French singer Amanda Lear, released by the West German label Ariola Records in 1979.
The album included notable hit singles "The Sphinx" and "Fashion Pack (Studio 54)", and turned out a commercial and critical success.
"The Pretty Things Are Going to Hell" is a song written by David Bowie and Reeves Gabrels for the album "Hours" in 1999.
The first single release from the album in Australia and Japan, while the rest of the world got "Thursday's Child" as their first single.
The first appearance of the song was on the soundtrack of the film "Stigmata" in 1999.
It charted and peaked at No. 30 in Japan.
The song's title takes influence from the song "Your Pretty Face Is Going to Hell" by The Stooges from their album "Raw Power" produced by Bowie himself.
Pretty Face (プリティフェイス , Puriti Feisu ) is a Japanese manga series written and illustrated by Japanese author Yasuhiro Kano.

The story revolves around a high school boy who suffers an accident and having his face reconstructed in the form of his crush, attempts to impersonate a girl whom his crush mistakes for her twin sister.
A common theme in the series is the loving relationships the characters share for each other, whether they be between siblings, friends, or lovers as well as Karma.
It was first serialized in Jump Comic's "Weekly Shōnen Jump" by Shueisha between May 2002 and June 2003, finally coming to an end at chapter fifty-two.
For North America Viz Media acquired the rights to release an English translation of the series.

title: She's Not Just a Pretty Face
Forever and for Always

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Who was the singer of She's Not Just a Pretty Face and Forever and for Always?
hop1_docs: She's Not Just a Pretty Face | "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain.  It was the fourth country single from her album "Up! ".  The song was written by Robert John "Mutt" Lange and Twain.  Twain had announced that "Nah!"  would be the next single after "Forever and for Always", but "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was released instead.  It became Twain's 15th top ten single on Billboard Country.  The song was originally to be released to the UK as a pop single on March 24, 2004, but that plan was cancelled.  It was however released to Eastern Europe as the fourth single, where it did reasonably well in some countries.  "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" debuted in North America on October 6, 2003.  Despite reaching the top ten, "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was not included in Twain's 2004 "Greatest Hits" CD.  The song was also nominated for Best Female Country Vocal Performance at the 2005 Grammy Awards.  The song was also performed on some highly rated TV shows, "The Oprah Winfrey Show", the 2003 Country Music Awards, and the 2003 Billboard Music Awards, all helping it land in the top ten.

Just a Pretty Face? | Just a Pretty Face?  is the third studio album by Finnish singer Anna Abreu, released in Finland by RCA on October 21, 2009.  The album was preceded by the lead single "Music Everywhere" and followed by the promotional singles "Impatient" and "Slam".
Not Just a Pretty Face (Rowan Atkinson album) | Not Just a Pretty Face is a comedy album by British comedian Rowan Atkinson.

Not Just Another Bunch of Pretty Faces | Not Just Another Bunch of Pretty Faces is the seventh studio album by British jazz-rock band If, released in 1974.  The band was now back on Capitol Records for U.S. distribution.
Always is Forever Tour | Always is Forever Tour was a concerts tour by American singer Diana Ross in 1999.

Always & Forever (Randy Travis album) | Always & Forever is the second studio album by country music singer Randy Travis.  It was released on April 4, 1987 by Warner Bros.  Records.  Released from this album were the singles "Too Gone Too Long", "I Won't Need You Anymore (Always and Forever)", "Forever and Ever, Amen" and "I Told You So", all of which reached Number One on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs charts.

Always and Forever (Kostas Martakis song) | "Always and Forever" is a song and CD single by Greek singer Kostas Martakis.  It was Martakis's entry in a race to be the Greek representative for the Eurovision Song Contest 2008.

hop2_docs: She's Not Just a Pretty Face | "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain.  It was the fourth country single from her album "Up! ".  The song was written by Robert John "Mutt" Lange and Twain.  Twain had announced that "Nah!"  would be the next single after "Forever and for Always", but "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was released instead.  It became Twain's 15th top ten single on Billboard Country.  The song was originally to be released to the UK as a pop single on March 24, 2004, but that plan was cancelled.  It was however released to Eastern Europe as the fourth single, where it did reasonably well in some countries.  "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" debuted in North America on October 6, 2003.  Despite reaching the top ten, "She's Not Just a Pretty Face" was not included in Twain's 2004 "Greatest Hits" CD.  The song was also nominated for Best Female Country Vocal Performance at the 2005 Grammy Awards.  The song was also performed on some highly rated TV shows, "The Oprah Winfrey Show", the 2003 Country Music Awards, and the 2003 Billboard Music Awards, all helping it land in the top ten.

Forever and for Always | "Forever and for Always" is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian country music singer Shania Twain and it was the third country single from her fourth studio album "Up! " (2002).  The song was written by Mutt Lange and Twain.  The single was released in the United States radio on April 7, 2003.  The video debuted on Country Music Television on April 26, 2003.  The song was also used for Febreze: Scentstories promotion, in which Twain took part.  In 2006, "Forever and for Always" was certified gold for 500,000 digital downloads in the U.S. by the RIAA.

Why Not? with Shania Twain | Why Not?  with Shania Twain is an American reality television series starring Canadian country music singer/songwriter Shania Twain.  It premiered on the Oprah Winfrey Network on May 8, 2011.  The series sheds light on the life of Twain, who by the age of 22, had survived a childhood of poverty and the loss of both her parents in an accident.  At the height of super-stardom, Twain's marriage ended.  In the series, Twain opens up about her personal tragedies and how she dealt with them.  Also interviewed are her sister, friends, bandmates, and others in the music industry.  She marries her fiancé during the concluding episode.

Shania Twain (album) | Shania Twain is the self-titled debut studio album by Shania Twain, released on April 20, 1993.  Although not a hit when originally released, Twain's subsequent successful albums gained her legions of fans, which generated more interest in "Shania Twain" causing it to be certified platinum in the US for shipments of one million copies.  None of the album's songs were included in Twain's "Greatest Hits" album, released in 2004.

Shania Twain videography | This is a list of Shania Twain music videos.  The list contains all thirty three music videos, with their respective directors, filming locations and release dates.  Also included is a list of alternate versions of each music video as Twain's music appeals to both country and pop markets.  All of the videos released from "Shania Twain", "The Woman in Me" and "Come on Over" are available on Twain's "Platinum Collection" DVD, with the exception of "When".  Videos from "Up!"  and "Greatest Hits" are available on select enhanced CD singles and a special DVD-Audio version of "Up!" .  Many of Twain's music videos have won various awards, see Shania Twain awards for more information.

Shania Twain Centre | The Shania Twain Centre was a city-owned tourist attraction that operated in Timmins, Ontario from 2001 to 2013.  It exhibited memorabilia, awards and other personal items loaned to the Centre by singer Shania Twain, who was raised in Timmins.

I'm Gonna Getcha Good! | "I'm Gonna Getcha Good!"  is a song co-written and recorded by Canadian singer Shania Twain.  It was the first single from her album "Up! " The song was written by Mutt Lange and Shania Twain.  "I'm Gonna Getcha Good!"  was originally released to American radio on September 23, 2002.  Shania chose "I'm Gonna Getcha Good!"  as the first single for "Up!"  since she felt it was relatable to past Shania singles, and she didn't want something too unfamiliar.  The song has become one of her biggest hits worldwide, charting in different countries that she has never had hits in before.  At the 2003 Juno Awards "I'm Gonna Getcha Good!"  was named Country Recording of the Year.  Later, the Jonas Brothers covered the song for the soundtrack for their film "".
